sightWords.question: Which word means the opposite of 'hot'?
sightWords.fact: 'Cold' is the opposite of 'hot' — these are called antonyms, words that mean opposite things!

sightWords.question: Which of these is a colour word?
sightWords.fact: 'Blue' is a colour word! Colour words describe what things look like.

sightWords.question: What does the word 'big' mean?
sightWords.fact: 'Big' means large in size — the opposite of big is small or little!

sightWords.question: Which word is a number?
sightWords.fact: 'Three' is a number word — number words tell us how many things there are!

sightWords.question: Which sentence uses 'the' correctly?
sightWords.fact: 'The' is one of the most common words in English — it points to a specific thing!

sightWords.question: Which word means to move quickly on your legs?
sightWords.fact: 'Run' means to move your legs quickly — faster than walking!

sightWords.question: Which word is the opposite of 'up'?
sightWords.fact: 'Down' is the opposite of 'up' — these direction words tell us where things are!

sightWords.question: Which word means you and me together?
sightWords.fact: 'We' means 'you and I' together — it is a pronoun that talks about a group including yourself!

sightWords.question: Which word means to use your eyes?
sightWords.fact: 'See' means to use your eyes — our five senses each have their own special word!

sightWords.question: Which word is an animal?
sightWords.fact: 'Cat' is a noun — a word for a living thing, place or object!

sightWords.question: Which word means 'not any'?
sightWords.fact: 'No' means none at all — it is one of the first words children learn to read!

sightWords.question: Which word tells us WHERE something is?
sightWords.fact: 'Here' is a place word — it tells us where something is happening!

sightWords.question: Which word means belonging to me?
sightWords.fact: 'My' means it belongs to me — 'your' means it belongs to you!

sightWords.question: What does 'play' mean?
sightWords.fact: 'Play' means to take part in games or fun activities — it is something children do every day!

sightWords.question: Which word means 'a lot'?
sightWords.fact: 'Many' means a large number of things — the opposite of many is few!

sightWords.question: Which word means happy and cheerful?
sightWords.fact: 'Glad' means happy or pleased — it is a synonym for happy!

sightWords.question: Which word is a day of the week?
sightWords.fact: Monday is a day of the week — there are seven days: Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, Friday, Saturday, Sunday!

sightWords.question: Which word comes BEFORE a vowel sound?
sightWords.fact: We use 'an' before a vowel sound — like 'an apple' or 'an egg'. We use 'a' before consonant sounds!

phonics.question: What sound does the letter 'b' make?
phonics.fact: The letter 'b' makes a buzzing sound — like 'b-b-ball'! It is made with your lips together.

phonics.question: Which word starts with the 'sh' sound?
phonics.fact: 'Sh' is a digraph — two letters that make one sound together, like in 'ship' and 'shell'!

phonics.question: How many syllables does 'butterfly' have?
phonics.fact: 'But-ter-fly' has 3 syllables! Clap once for each syllable — but-ter-fly!

phonics.question: Which word rhymes with 'cat'?
phonics.fact: 'Cat' and 'hat' rhyme — they end with the same sound 'at'!

phonics.question: What sound do the letters 'ch' make together?
phonics.fact: 'Ch' makes the sound in 'chair', 'cheese' and 'chop' — it is a digraph!

phonics.question: Which letter is silent in 'knight'?
phonics.fact: The 'k' in 'knight' is silent — we do not pronounce it! Silent letters are hidden in many English words.

phonics.question: Which word starts with a vowel?
phonics.fact: 'Apple' starts with the vowel 'a'. The vowels are a, e, i, o and u!

phonics.question: What sound does 'ph' make in 'phone'?
phonics.fact: 'Ph' makes an 'f' sound — like in 'phone', 'photo' and 'dolphin'!

phonics.question: Which word has a long 'a' sound?
phonics.fact: 'Cake' has a long 'a' sound — 'a-a-cake'! The magic 'e' at the end makes the vowel say its name!

phonics.question: How many sounds are in the word 'ship'?
phonics.fact: 'Ship' has 3 sounds: 'sh' + 'i' + 'p' — even though it has 4 letters, 'sh' is one sound!

phonics.question: Which word ends with the 'ng' sound?
phonics.fact: 'Sing' ends with the 'ng' sound — like 'ring', 'king' and 'song'!

phonics.question: What are the five vowels in English?
phonics.fact: The five vowels are a, e, i, o, u — every word has at least one vowel!

phonics.question: Which word rhymes with 'night'?
phonics.fact: 'Night' and 'light' rhyme — they share the 'ight' sound at the end!

phonics.question: Which word has the 'oo' sound as in 'moon'?
phonics.fact: 'Food' has the long 'oo' sound — like in 'moon', 'spoon' and 'zoo'!

phonics.question: What does a blend mean in phonics?
options: Two consonants that keep their own sounds when spoken together
phonics.fact: A blend is two consonants together where you hear both sounds — like 'bl' in 'blue' or 'st' in 'stop'!

phonics.question: Which word starts with a blend?
phonics.fact: 'Star' starts with the 'st' blend — you can hear both the 's' and 't' sounds!

phonics.question: How many syllables does 'elephant' have?
phonics.fact: 'El-e-phant' has 3 syllables! Tap your knee once for each part: el-e-phant!

phonics.question: Which word uses the 'magic e' rule?
phonics.fact: 'Hope' uses magic e — the 'e' at the end makes the 'o' say its name: h-O-pe, not hop!

spelling.question: Which spelling is correct?
spelling.fact: 'Cat' is the correct spelling — c-a-t! Cats are the most popular pet in the world.

spelling.question: Which word is spelled correctly?
spelling.fact: 'House' is spelled h-o-u-s-e. Remember the silent 'e' at the end!

spelling.question: How do you spell the colour of the sky on a sunny day?
spelling.fact: 'Blue' is spelled b-l-u-e. The 'ue' together makes the 'oo' sound!

spelling.question: Which word is spelled correctly?
spelling.fact: 'Family' is spelled f-a-m-i-l-y. Remember: one 'm' and one 'l'!

spelling.question: Which is the correct spelling?
spelling.fact: 'Elephant' is spelled e-l-e-p-h-a-n-t. Remember 'ph' makes an 'f' sound!

spelling.question: How do you spell a day after today?
spelling.fact: 'Tomorrow' has a double 'r' — t-o-m-o-r-r-o-w. Don't forget the double r!

spelling.question: Which word is spelled correctly?
spelling.fact: 'Friend' is spelled f-r-i-e-n-d. A tricky one — remember 'i before e'!

spelling.question: Which is the correct spelling for a season?
spelling.fact: 'Winter' is spelled w-i-n-t-e-r — the coldest season of the year!

spelling.question: Which word is spelled correctly?
spelling.fact: 'Because' is spelled b-e-c-a-u-s-e. Try this: Big Elephants Can Always Use Small Exits!

spelling.question: How do you spell a number between 1 and 3?
spelling.fact: 'Two' is the number 2 — spelled t-w-o. 'Too' means also, and 'to' is a direction word!

spelling.question: Which word is spelled correctly?
spelling.fact: 'Christmas' is spelled C-h-r-i-s-t-m-a-s — don't forget the silent 't'!

spelling.question: Which is the correct spelling?
spelling.fact: 'Different' has a double 'f' — d-i-f-f-e-r-e-n-t. Remember two f's!

spelling.question: Which word is spelled correctly?
spelling.fact: 'Library' is spelled l-i-b-r-a-r-y — don't forget the first 'r'!

spelling.question: Which is correct?
spelling.fact: 'Birthday' is spelled b-i-r-t-h-d-a-y. It has 'birth' + 'day' joined together!

spelling.question: Which word is spelled correctly?
spelling.fact: 'Wednesday' is spelled W-e-d-n-e-s-d-a-y — there is a hidden 'd' in the middle!

spelling.question: How do you spell the number after nine?
spelling.fact: 'Ten' is spelled t-e-n — a short, simple word for the number 10!

spelling.question: Which word is spelled correctly?
spelling.fact: 'Beautiful' is spelled b-e-a-u-t-i-f-u-l — a beautiful long word with lots of vowels!

spelling.question: Which is the correct spelling for what a doctor does?
spelling.fact: 'Examine' is spelled e-x-a-m-i-n-e — it means to look at something very carefully!

sentences.question: Which of these is a complete sentence?
sentences.fact: A complete sentence has a subject (who) and a verb (action) — 'The big dog ran fast' has both!

sentences.question: What punctuation goes at the end of a question?
sentences.fact: Questions end with a question mark (?) — it shows we are asking something!

sentences.question: Which sentence starts correctly?
sentences.fact: Every sentence starts with a capital letter — 'The cat sat.' is correct!

sentences.question: What is the subject of: 'The bird sings.'?
sentences.fact: The subject is who or what the sentence is about — 'The bird' is doing the singing!

sentences.question: Which word is the verb (action) in: 'She jumps high.'?
sentences.fact: 'Jumps' is the verb — it tells us what she is doing! Verbs are action words.

sentences.question: What punctuation ends an exciting sentence?
sentences.fact: Exclamation marks (!) show excitement or strong feelings — like 'We won!'

sentences.question: Which sentence is correct?
sentences.fact: 'I have a dog' is correct — 'have' is the right verb to use with 'I'!

sentences.question: What does a comma (,) do in a list?
sentences.fact: Commas separate items in a list — like 'apples, oranges, and bananas'!

sentences.question: Which sentence uses 'and' correctly?
sentences.fact: 'And' joins two ideas together — 'I like cats and dogs' joins two things you like!

sentences.question: What type of sentence is: 'Please sit down.'?
sentences.fact: A command (imperative sentence) tells someone to do something — it often starts with a verb!

sentences.question: Which sentence is in the past tense?
sentences.fact: 'She ran fast' is past tense — 'ran' tells us it already happened!

sentences.question: What is an adjective?
sentences.fact: Adjectives describe nouns — like 'The big red ball'. 'Big' and 'red' are adjectives!

sentences.question: Which word is a noun in: 'The happy dog barked.'?
sentences.fact: 'Dog' is a noun — nouns are naming words for people, places, animals and things!

sentences.question: Which sentence needs a capital letter fixed?
sentences.fact: 'tom likes pizza.' needs fixing — always start a sentence with a capital letter!

sentences.question: What is a statement?
sentences.fact: A statement is a sentence that tells or declares something — like 'The sun is bright.'

sentences.question: Which sentence uses 'they' correctly?
sentences.fact: 'They are happy' is correct — use 'are' with 'they', 'we', and 'you'!

sentences.question: What does a full stop (.) do?
sentences.fact: A full stop (or period) ends a statement sentence — it shows the idea is complete!

sentences.question: Which word is an adverb in: 'She sings beautifully.'?
sentences.fact: 'Beautifully' is an adverb — it describes HOW she sings. Adverbs often end in '-ly'!

rhymingWords.question: Which word rhymes with 'dog'?
rhymingWords.fact: 'Dog' and 'log' rhyme — they both end with the 'og' sound!

rhymingWords.question: Which word rhymes with 'sun'?
rhymingWords.fact: 'Sun' and 'fun' rhyme — they both end with the 'un' sound!

rhymingWords.question: Which word rhymes with 'cake'?
rhymingWords.fact: 'Cake' and 'bake' rhyme — they share the 'ake' sound at the end!

rhymingWords.question: Which word rhymes with 'tree'?
rhymingWords.fact: 'Tree' and 'bee' rhyme — they both end with the long 'ee' sound!

rhymingWords.question: Which word rhymes with 'hop'?
rhymingWords.fact: 'Hop' and 'top' rhyme — they share the 'op' sound!

rhymingWords.question: Which word rhymes with 'rain'?
rhymingWords.fact: 'Rain' and 'train' rhyme — they both end with the 'ain' sound!

rhymingWords.question: Which word rhymes with 'big'?
rhymingWords.fact: 'Big' and 'pig' rhyme — they share the 'ig' sound!

rhymingWords.question: Which word rhymes with 'hand'?
rhymingWords.fact: 'Hand' and 'sand' rhyme — they both end with the 'and' sound!

rhymingWords.question: Which word rhymes with 'flower'?
rhymingWords.fact: 'Flower' and 'power' rhyme — they share the 'ower' sound!

rhymingWords.question: Which word rhymes with 'sky'?
rhymingWords.fact: 'Sky' and 'high' rhyme — they both make the long 'I' sound!

rhymingWords.question: Which word rhymes with 'book'?
rhymingWords.fact: 'Book' and 'cook' rhyme — they share the 'ook' sound!

rhymingWords.question: Which word rhymes with 'night'?
rhymingWords.fact: 'Night' and 'light' rhyme — they both end with the 'ight' sound!

rhymingWords.question: Which word rhymes with 'fish'?
rhymingWords.fact: 'Fish' and 'dish' rhyme — they share the 'ish' sound!

rhymingWords.question: Which word rhymes with 'red'?
rhymingWords.fact: 'Red' and 'bed' rhyme — they both end with the 'ed' sound!

rhymingWords.question: What does it mean for two words to rhyme?
options: They start with the same letter
They end with the same sound
rhymingWords.fact: Rhyming words end with the same sound — like 'cat' and 'hat', or 'blue' and 'true'!

rhymingWords.question: Which word rhymes with 'sing'?
rhymingWords.fact: 'Sing' and 'ring' rhyme — they share the 'ing' sound!

rhymingWords.question: Which word rhymes with 'mouse'?
rhymingWords.fact: 'Mouse' and 'house' rhyme — they share the 'ouse' sound!

rhymingWords.question: Which pair of words rhymes?
rhymingWords.fact: 'Run' and 'sun' rhyme — they both end with the 'un' sound!

nounsVerbs.question: What is a noun?
options: A naming word for a person, place or thing
nounsVerbs.fact: Nouns are naming words — they name people, places, animals and things around us!

nounsVerbs.question: Which word is a noun?
nounsVerbs.fact: 'Apple' is a noun — it is the name of a fruit, which is a thing!

nounsVerbs.question: What is a verb?
nounsVerbs.fact: Verbs are action words — they tell us what someone or something does!

nounsVerbs.question: Which word is a verb?
nounsVerbs.fact: 'Swim' is a verb — it is an action! You can swim in a pool or the sea.

nounsVerbs.question: Which word is a proper noun (the name of a specific place)?
nounsVerbs.fact: 'London' is a proper noun — the specific name of a city. Proper nouns always start with a capital letter!

nounsVerbs.question: Which of these is a verb?
nounsVerbs.fact: 'Read' is a verb — it describes the action of looking at and understanding words!

nounsVerbs.question: In 'The dog barked loudly', what is the verb?
nounsVerbs.fact: 'Barked' is the verb — it tells us what the dog did! Past tense verbs often end in '-ed'.

nounsVerbs.question: In 'My sister plays football', what is a noun?
nounsVerbs.fact: 'Sister' is a noun — it names a person! 'Football' is also a noun (a thing).

nounsVerbs.question: Which of these is a common noun?
nounsVerbs.fact: 'Cat' is a common noun — the general name for that type of animal. 'Fido' would be a proper noun!

nounsVerbs.question: Which word is a verb?
nounsVerbs.fact: 'Fly' is a verb — it describes the action of moving through the air!

nounsVerbs.question: How many nouns are in: 'The cat sat on the mat'?
nounsVerbs.fact: 'Cat' and 'mat' are both nouns — two naming words! 'The' and 'on' are not nouns.

nounsVerbs.question: What is a plural noun?
nounsVerbs.fact: A plural noun names more than one thing — we usually add 's' or 'es', like cats, buses!

nounsVerbs.question: Which sentence has a verb?
nounsVerbs.fact: 'She reads books' has the verb 'reads' — it tells us what she does!

nounsVerbs.question: What type of word is 'quickly'?
nounsVerbs.fact: 'Quickly' is an adverb — it describes HOW you do something. Adverbs often end in '-ly'!

nounsVerbs.question: Which is a collective noun (a word for a group)?
nounsVerbs.fact: A 'flock' is a collective noun for a group of birds — like a 'herd' of cows or a 'school' of fish!

nounsVerbs.question: Which word can be both a noun AND a verb?
nounsVerbs.fact: 'Rain' can be a noun ('The rain fell') or a verb ('It will rain') — some words work as both!

nounsVerbs.question: What makes a noun plural in most cases?
nounsVerbs.fact: Most nouns become plural by adding 's' — one cat becomes two cats! Some add 'es' like buses.

nounsVerbs.question: Which is a verb that describes a sound?
nounsVerbs.fact: 'Bang' is a verb describing a sound — you can 'bang a drum'! It is also an onomatopoeia!

punctuation.question: What is a full stop used for?
punctuation.fact: A full stop (.) ends a statement sentence — it tells the reader the idea is complete!

punctuation.question: When do we use a question mark?
options: At the end of every sentence
At the end of a question
At the start of a sentence
punctuation.fact: A question mark (?) goes at the end of a question — like 'Where are you going?'

punctuation.question: What does an exclamation mark (!) show?
punctuation.fact: An exclamation mark shows strong emotions — like surprise, excitement or a command: 'Watch out!'

punctuation.question: What is the job of a capital letter?
options: To start a sentence or name a specific person or place
To separate items in a list
punctuation.fact: Capital letters start sentences and are used for proper nouns like names and places!

punctuation.question: What does a comma do in a list?
options: Separates items so we can read them clearly
punctuation.fact: Commas in lists separate each item — 'I bought apples, bananas, and grapes.'

punctuation.question: Which sentence uses punctuation correctly?
punctuation.fact: 'Where are you going?' has a capital letter at the start and a question mark at the end!

punctuation.question: What are inverted commas used for?
punctuation.fact: Inverted commas (speech marks) show the exact words someone says — like Tom said, "Hello!"

punctuation.question: Which name needs a capital letter?
punctuation.fact: 'Emma' needs a capital E — all people's names are proper nouns with capital letters!

punctuation.question: What punctuation is missing? 'My cat is called Whiskers_'
punctuation.fact: A full stop is needed — 'My cat is called Whiskers.' is a statement sentence!

punctuation.question: Which sentence needs an exclamation mark?
punctuation.fact: 'We won the game!' shows great excitement — an exclamation mark fits perfectly here!

punctuation.question: What do we call the mark at the end of 'Help!'?
punctuation.fact: The '!' is an exclamation mark — 'Help!' shows urgency and strong feeling!

punctuation.question: Which words in 'i love monday' need capital letters?
punctuation.fact: 'I' is always a capital letter, and 'Monday' is a proper noun — so both need capitals: 'I love Monday'!

punctuation.question: What does an apostrophe (') do in 'can't'?
punctuation.fact: An apostrophe in 'can't' shows letters are missing — 'can't' is short for 'cannot'!

punctuation.question: Which sentence is punctuated correctly?
punctuation.fact: 'She ran quickly.' has a capital 'S' to start and a full stop to end — perfectly punctuated!

punctuation.question: What kind of punctuation goes at the end of 'Look out'?
punctuation.fact: 'Look out!' needs an exclamation mark — it is a warning with a strong, urgent feeling!

punctuation.question: In 'London is a city', which word already has correct capitalisation?
punctuation.fact: 'London' already has a capital L — it is a proper noun, the name of a specific city!

punctuation.question: How many punctuation marks are in 'Run! Run fast!'?
punctuation.fact: There are 2 exclamation marks — one after each command. 'Run! Run fast!' has 2 punctuation marks.

punctuation.question: What does a colon (:) often introduce?
punctuation.fact: A colon introduces a list or explanation — like 'I need three things: a pen, paper and glue.'

readingComprehension.question: Tom has a red ball. He likes to kick it in the garden. What colour is Tom's ball?
readingComprehension.fact: The text says 'Tom has a red ball' — the answer is right there in the first sentence!

readingComprehension.question: Sara woke up early. She ate toast and then walked to school. What did Sara eat?
readingComprehension.fact: The text tells us Sara 'ate toast' — finding answers in the text is called locating information!

readingComprehension.question: The sky turned dark and it began to rain. The children ran inside. Why did the children run inside?
readingComprehension.fact: The text says it 'began to rain' — this caused the children to run inside! This is cause and effect.

readingComprehension.question: Ben has three cats: Fluffy, Spot and Tiger. How many cats does Ben have?
readingComprehension.fact: The text says 'three cats' — always look for exact numbers mentioned in the text!

readingComprehension.question: The old dog slept all day under the big oak tree. Where did the dog sleep?
readingComprehension.fact: The text tells us exactly where — 'under the big oak tree'. Specific details are important!

readingComprehension.question: Lily loves books. She reads every night before bed. When does Lily read?
readingComprehension.fact: The text says 'every night before bed' — reading for detail helps us find when things happen!

readingComprehension.question: A caterpillar eats leaves to grow. Then it makes a chrysalis. What does a caterpillar eat?
readingComprehension.fact: 'A caterpillar eats leaves to grow' — the answer is stated directly in the text!

readingComprehension.question: Jake was sad because he lost his toy. Later his mum found it. How did Jake feel at the start?
readingComprehension.fact: 'Jake was sad' — character feelings are important information to notice when reading!

readingComprehension.question: The sun sets in the west. In which direction does the sun set?
readingComprehension.fact: 'The sun sets in the west' — this is a fact stated in the text. The sun rises in the east!

readingComprehension.question: Amy painted her room yellow. She loves bright colours. What colour did Amy paint her room?
readingComprehension.fact: The text says Amy 'painted her room yellow' — always look for the specific detail mentioned!

readingComprehension.question: The shop was closed on Sunday. Mia went on Sunday. Could Mia shop?
readingComprehension.fact: If the shop was closed on Sunday and Mia went on Sunday — she could not shop! This is inference.

readingComprehension.question: Fish live in water and breathe through gills. What do fish use to breathe?
readingComprehension.fact: The text tells us fish 'breathe through gills' — gills work like lungs but underwater!

readingComprehension.question: Max ran as fast as he could but still missed the bus. Why was Max running?
options: He wanted to catch the bus
readingComprehension.fact: Max 'missed the bus' — we can infer he was rushing because he wanted to catch it!

readingComprehension.question: The title of a book tells us what it is about. What is the purpose of a book's title?
options: To tell us what the book is about
To show when it was written
readingComprehension.fact: A title gives us a clue about the topic — it helps us predict what we will read!

readingComprehension.question: Rosa fed her rabbit every morning before school. How often did Rosa feed her rabbit?
readingComprehension.fact: 'Every morning before school' — the text tells us exactly how often!

readingComprehension.question: The word 'enormous' means very, very big. What does enormous mean?
readingComprehension.fact: The text defines 'enormous' for us — using context clues in the text helps us learn new words!

readingComprehension.question: At the end of the story, the lost puppy found its owner. How did the story end?
options: The owner bought a new puppy
readingComprehension.fact: Identifying how a story ends helps us understand the conclusion!

readingComprehension.question: A glossary is found at the back of non-fiction books. What is a glossary?
options: A list of difficult words and their meanings
A list of the author's books
readingComprehension.fact: A glossary explains tricky words — it is like a mini-dictionary at the back of the book!
